findings.finding: The graph outcome, three observable objectives, six-part explanatory loop, modeled plan, practice operations, and four assessment items describe the same core performances: distinguish exposure from retrieval evidence, construct a bounded plan, and adapt the next opportunity from observed performance. Terminology, assumptions, and limits remain stable across those stages.

The independent Noor task appropriately removes prompts, but its post-attempt feedback supplies only a self-evaluation checklist. A foundation learner can answer each checklist question affirmatively while still producing an underspecified sequence that does not show how a particular graph-reading error changes the next retrieval opportunity. The earlier complete worked example limits the consequence, so this is a bounded feedback-support issue rather than invalid alignment.
findings.resolution: After the learner attempts and revises from the checklist, add one concise exemplar plan or a criterion-by-criterion worked fragment that demonstrates how Noor's word-cue error leads to contrasting-case recovery, renewed retrieval, and a changed next opportunity. Keep it after the attempt so retrieval remains genuine.

findings.finding: The practice transfer coordinates four differing baselines and peer feedback, while the scored transfer changes the learner configuration, communication constraint, error patterns, retention horizon, final performance, and required control-selection decision. The assessment therefore requires selection and adaptation rather than replay of the displayed allocation. The 16-of-20 threshold plus 4-of-6 floors on plan design, adaptation, and transfer prevents recognition success from compensating for failure on the claimed constructed performances.

The complete model exposes givens, constraints, model selection, planning decisions, execution, checks, interpretation, and self-explanation; later prompts precede answer reveal. The misconception sequence elicits a prediction, contrasts the plausible fluency model with observable performance, repairs it neutrally, and rechecks the distinction in a changed case before support fades through completion, supported, independent, mixed, and transfer tasks.

limitations: This was a learning-design review only; academic source verification and accessibility-and-rights determinations were outside this role.

No direct learner observation, delayed retention data, or controlled usability study was available, so the review evaluates the instructional argument and mastery evidence design rather than measured effectiveness.

The review used the canonical source pack and deterministic validation; it did not add a separate browser-reader or assistive-technology test beyond the source-level continuity and alternative-route audit.
